burial.plot_notes: Mount Pleasant Cemetery Toronto — with husband James Botterell Redpath.
Mount Pleasant Cemetery Toronto — with husband James Botterell Redpath.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of James Botterell Redpath (1908-1992), President of Dome Mines Limited.

career.notes: President of Dome Mines Limited 1959-1977. Dome Mines was a major Canadian gold mining company.

narrative_blocks.header: Dome Mines president, eighteen years at the top
narrative_blocks.body: James Botterell Redpath spent his career in Canadian mining, ultimately serving as President of Dome Mines Limited — a major Canadian gold mining company — from 1959 to 1977. He raised his family at Val-d'Or in northern Quebec where his son James Stockton Redpath grew up around mining operations. The family business orientation extended forward when his son founded J.S. Redpath Ltd., a global leader in mining contracting services.

narrative_blocks.header: He raised his family at the minehead
narrative_blocks.body: He and Margaret raised three children at Val-d’Or among the mining operations — one founded the J.S. Redpath contracting firm and took the Order of Ontario, one went to the World Bank, one to a Stanford anesthesiology career.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of James Stockton 'Jim' Redpath (1936-2025), founder of J.S. Redpath Ltd.

notable.notable_blurb: Founded JS Redpath Ltd. mining contractor, Order of

education.notes: McGill University degree in mining engineering.
Studied art curation at Sotheby's New York after leaving the mining industry at age 50.

career.notes: Founded J.S. Redpath Ltd. at age 26 — mining contracting company specializing in shaft sinking. Built it into a global leader in mining services before stepping away at age 50.
Led the Capitol Arts Centre North Bay for several years after leaving mining; supported local artists and cultural growth.
Served as the first Chancellor of Nipissing University in 1994.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded global mining contractor at twenty-six
narrative_blocks.body: James Stockton 'Jim' Redpath grew up at a mine site in Val-d'Or Quebec where his father James Botterell Redpath worked for Dome Mines. After McGill mining engineering and two formative summers on a Hudson Bay exploration boat as a teenager, he founded J.S. Redpath Limited at age twenty-six — a shaft-sinking mining contractor that became a global leader in mining services. He stepped away from the company at fifty to pursue art curation studies at Sotheby's and lead the Capitol Arts Centre in North Bay.

narrative_blocks.header: Order of Ontario, Queen's Jubilee Medals, two doctorates
narrative_blocks.body: Jim Redpath received honorary doctorates from both McGill University and Nipissing University, served as the first Chancellor of Nipissing in 1994, and was awarded Queen Elizabeth II's Golden and Diamond Jubilee Medals along with the Order of Ontario. He retired with his wife Bobbi to St. John's Newfoundland — closing a family arc that began with his great-great-uncle Sir Thomas Roddick's birth at Harbour Grace Newfoundland in 1846. He died there in 2025 after a twenty-year journey with Parkinson's Disease.

education.notes: One of the first women to receive an MBA from York University.

career.notes: Started career with World Bank in Washington.
Helped small growing companies as business consultant.
Long-time committed advisory board member of Inanna Publications and its academic journal Canadian Woman Studies.

naming_inspiration.description: First name Florence honors paternal grandmother Florence Amelia Botterell Redpath.

narrative_blocks.header: Among first women MBAs at York, World Bank
narrative_blocks.body: Florence Anne 'Anne' Redpath grew up in Val-d'Or northern Quebec and graduated from McGill University. She was one of the first women to receive an MBA from York University, then started her career with the World Bank in Washington before returning to Canada to work on public and private research projects and eventually as a business consultant. She was an ardent supporter of York's School of Women's Studies and a long-time advisory board member of Inanna Publications and Canadian Woman Studies.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of John Huntly Redpath (1942-2024), Stanford-trained anesthesiologist.

notable.notable_blurb: Stanford-trained anesthesiologist, Palo Alto Medical Foundation

education.notes: Attended Bishop's College School; was Head Prefect.
Began anesthesia residency Calgary AB 1975; completed at Stanford.

career.notes: Partner at Palo Alto Medical Foundation 1977 until retirement 1998. Also served on Medical Board of Stanford Hospital. Active in Interplast (international plastic surgery charity).
Internship Toronto then opened family practice. Changed course in 1975 to anesthesia.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Huntly carries his uncle Huntly Roddick Redpath's first name into the next generation — Huntly was John Huntly's father James Botterell's brother.

narrative_blocks.header: Princeton, McGill MD, Stanford anesthesia partner
narrative_blocks.body: John Huntly Redpath was born at Val-d'Or northern Quebec where his father James Botterell Redpath ran Dome Mines operations. After Bishop's College School (Head Prefect), Princeton 1964, and McGill MD 1968, he practiced family medicine in Toronto before changing course to anesthesia in 1975 — completing his residency at Stanford and joining the Palo Alto Medical Foundation in 1977 where he remained a partner for twenty-one years. He died in Palo Alto in March 2024 after a long fight with aggressive cancer.

The Palo Alto anesthesiologist worked with Interplast, the international reconstructive-surgery charity, and sat on Stanford Hospital’s medical board. He retired in 1998 to Angels Camp and a calendar of kayaks, ducks, and golf.

burialCemetery.name: Mount Pleasant Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Mount Pleasant Cemetery, Toronto Ontario. Established 1876, major Canadian Victorian cemetery. Burial site of James Botterell Redpath (HD4197, President of Dome Mines Ltd 1959-1977) and his wife Margaret Isobel Stockton Redpath.
